data.title: Metaplanet Buys 319 More Bitcoin; Lifts Holdings to 4,525
data.description:
Metaplanet bought 319 Bitcoin for 3.78 billion yen between April 1 and 14, raising its total holdings to 4,525 BTC, according to a Monday filing on the Tokyo Stock Exchange.

The average purchase price was 11.85 million yen per Bitcoin.

The firm has now spent 58.1 billion yen on Bitcoin at an average of 12.85 million yen each.

Quarter-to-date, its BTC Yield - a measure of bitcoin per fully diluted share - rose 6.5%, translating to a 263 BTC gain, valued at 3.17 billion yen.

Metaplanet shares were down over 1% in recent trade.

data.title: Wilmar Responds to Reports of Indonesian Judge Arrests in Bribery Case
data.description:
Wilmar International has issued a statement addressing recent media reports concerning the arrest of Indonesian judges involved in a 2022 bribery case where the company was implicated.

Indonesian authorities have reportedly apprehended judges who were part of the panel that acquitted Wilmar International, along with Musim Mas group and Permata Hijau group, on charges of misconduct related to the acquisition of palm oil export permits in 2022.

Wilmar International said it is aware of the developments but reiterated its stance on the original charges, according to a filing with the Singapore Exchange on Monday.

data.title: British Business - April 15
data.description:
The following are the top stories on the business pages of British newspapers. Reuters has not verified these stories and does not vouch for their accuracy.

The Times

- The chief executive of Goldman Sachs has become the latest Wall Street boss to sound the alarm over President Trump's tariffs, warning that the tit-for-tat trade war poses "material risks" to the U.S. and world economy.

- The American semiconductor group Nvidia will spend up to $500 billion building supercomputers for artificial intelligence entirely in the U.S. for the first time, as it becomes the latest U.S. technology firm to bow to the Trump administration's push for domestic manufacturing.

The Guardian

- The big American tech firms known as the "Silicon Six"- Amazon , Meta , Alphabet , Netflix , Apple and Microsoft have been accused of paying almost $278 billion (211 billion pounds) less corporate income tax in the past decade compared with the statutory rate for U.S. companies making the same profits.

- The Sheilas' Wheels owner, Esure, will be sold to the Belgian insurer Ageas in a 1.3 billion pound deal where the UK insurer will be bought from the private equity firm Bain Capital in a deal funded through a combination of surplus cash and debt or equity.

The Telegraph

- U.S. President Donald Trump has vowed to hit imports of pharmaceuticals with new tariffs in a major setback for one of Britain's largest export industries.

Sky News

- The UK will announce a 120 million pound aid package for Sudan at a conference in London to mark the two-year anniversary of the conflict in the African country.

data.title: Hainan Airport Infrastructure's 2024 Profit Declines 52%; Shares Slip 3%
data.description:
Hainan Airport Infrastructure attributable profit fell 52% to 458.5 million yuan in 2024 from 952.8 million yuan in 2023, according to an annual report filed Tuesday with the Shanghai bourse.

The airport operator's earnings per share decreased 52% to 0.0401 yuan from 0.083 yuan in the previous year.

Operating income declined 35% to 4.37 billion yuan from 6.76 billion yuan a year earlier.

The airport operator's shares fell 3% during Tuesday morning trading.

data.title: Ageas Plans to Issue EUR525 Million of Shares to Partly Fund Esure Deal
data.description:
Ageas launched an accelerated bookbuild offering of newly issued ordinary shares to raise 525 million euros.

The Belgian insurer said in a Monday release that the offering is meant to partly finance its acquisition of UK-based digital personal lines insurer Esure for a cash consideration of 1.30 billion pounds sterling.

data.title: Star Posts Large Loss in Delayed First-Half Result
data.description:
(02:24 GMT) *Star Entertainment 1H Statutory Net Loss A$301.9 Mln
(02:24 GMT) *Star Entertainment 1H Normalized Net Loss A$135.7 Mln
(02:24 GMT) *Star Entertainment 1H Revenue A$724 Mln, Down 16.4%
(02:25 GMT) *Star Entertainment 1H Loss Before Interest, Tax and Significant Items A$57.3 Mln
(02:26 GMT) *Star Entertainment: Trading Performance Further Deteriorated Over 1H
(02:27 GMT) *Star Entertainment: Mandatory Carded Play, Cash Limits, Casino Operating Reforms and Market Share Loss Impacted 1H
(02:28 GMT) *Star Entertainment: Softening in Trading During 3Q, With Revenue Declining 9% Vs 2Q
(02:29 GMT) *Star Entertainment: Available Cash A$98 Mln as of April 11
(02:30 GMT) *Star Entertainment: Remains Material Uncertainty Regarding Group's Ability to Continue as Going Concern
(02:31 GMT) *Star Entertainment: Completing Bally's-Led A$300 Mln Strategic Investment Critical to Liquidity Outlook
(02:33 GMT) *Star Entertainment: Pursuing Range of Initiatives to Improve Performance and Enhance Liquidity Position
(02:33 GMT) *Star Entertainment: Shareholder Meeting Regarding Bally's Investment Targeted for Late June
(02:34 GMT) *Star Entertainment: Targeting Completion of Transaction to Exit Destination Brisbane Consortium by End of June
(02:35 GMT) *Star Entertainment: 1H Normalized Revenue A$650 Mln, Down 25%
(02:36 GMT) *Star Entertainment: No Dividend Declared
(03:15 GMT) Star Posts Large Loss in Delayed First-Half Result

By Mike Cherney

SYDNEY--Struggling Australian casino operator Star Entertainment posted a large loss in its delayed first-half results and warned that trading conditions have continued to deteriorate as it tries to stay in business.

Star posted a statutory net loss of 302 million Australian dollars, equivalent to US$191.1 million, in the half year ended December. Stripping out one-time items, it said its normalized loss was A$136 million.

It said normalized revenue was A$650 million, a 25% drop, and that its normalized Ebitda loss was A$26 million, as previously disclosed in quarterly reports. No dividend was declared. It said its performance in the fiscal first half was affected by mandatory carded play and cash limits at its Sydney casino, as well as casino operating reforms and market-share loss at its Gold Coast casino.

Star also flagged that there had been a softening in trading in the fiscal third quarter, with revenue declining by 9% across the group compared with the second quarter.

The company said it is working to progress various transactions that are crucial for its ability to keep operating. That includes a A$300 million strategic investment led by Bally's, which Star said would be subject to a shareholder meeting in late June. It said it also aims to complete a deal with its joint-venture partners to exit the Destination Brisbane Consortium by the end of June.

Star was scheduled to report its half-year financial results at the end of February but said it couldn't do so given the financial uncertainty. Its shares have been suspended from trading since then.

Write to Mike Cherney at mike.cherney@wsj.com

data.title: South Korea Boosts Chip-Industry Support Amid U.S. Uncertainties
data.description:
By Timothy W. Martin

South Korea is readying roughly $23 billion in government support to aid its chip sector, which faces tariff pressure from the U.S. Semiconductors represented about one-fifth of the country's exports in 2024, led by homegrown firms like Samsung Electronics and SK Hynix, a key partner to Nvidia.

Facing "increased uncertainties" with the U.S., Seoul officials said Tuesday they would boost the government's chip investment to 33 trillion won, or the equivalent of $23.2 billion, a boost from a prior pledge of 26 trillion won. The funds cover infrastructure-construction costs, low-interest loans to companies and worker recruitment. At least 4 trillion won will be spent by next year.

This item is part of a Wall Street Journal live coverage event. The full stream can be found by searching P/WSJL (WSJ Live Coverage).

data.title: News Highlights: Top Global Markets News of the Day - Monday at 11 PM ET
data.description:
Fed's Waller says interest rates may need to be cut soon if largest Trump tariffs stay in place

If President Donald Trump's large tariffs remain in place for some time, the economy is likely to slow to a crawl, Federal Reserve governor Chris Waller said Monday. As a result, interest-rate cuts would be warranted, even if the taxes on imports cause a spike in inflation.

Asian Stock Markets Rise Amid Hopes for More U.S. Tariff Exemptions

Asian stock markets rose Tuesday morning amid tentative hopes for more U.S. tariff exemptions that could lessen the negative impact of tougher trade barriers.

The U.S. Corporate Bond Market Needs a Boost. Big Banks Are Helping.

Just over $15 billion worth of investment-grade bonds have been issued in April, the slowest start to the month since 2009.

S&P 500 tallies its first 'death cross' in 3 years. Here's what happens next.

The S&P 500 reached an ominous-sounding milestone on Monday, even as stocks largely added to their gains from last week's rebound.

OPEC Cuts Oil Demand Outlook Amid U.S. Tariff Hit

The Organization of the Petroleum Exporting Countries cut its forecast for oil demand growth just days after a surprise decision to boost output, citing the impact of U.S. tariffs on the global economy and crude consumption.

Trump's Trade War Is Threatening to Derail the Office-Market Recovery

Leasing by businesses hit a recent high in the first quarter, but economic uncertainty is prompting a pause in some dealmaking.

At China's Wholesale Hub, U.S. Orders Have Suddenly Halted. One Example: Socks.

Chinese sellers remain confident despite higher tariffs, saying it no longer makes sense to sell everyday goods to Americans.

German Exports to U.S. Hit Highest in Two Decades

The uptick could be short-lived given the Trump administration's raising of tariff barriers on imports from Europe.

ECB to Cut Rates Again as Tariffs Darken Growth Outlook

The case for a cut has been bolstered by the surprising strength of the euro in the wake of the April 2 tariff announcements.

Canadian Property Market Hurt by Tariff Concerns

The nascent recovery in Canada's housing market has become a casualty of the trade dispute with the U.S.

data.title: News Highlights: Top Company News of the Day - Monday at 11 PM ET
data.description:
Bank Trading Desks Are Minting Money From Trump's Tariff Chaos

Goldman Sachs, JPMorgan and Morgan Stanley all saw huge jumps in trading revenue.

Johnson & Johnson Is Beating the Market. Legal Troubles Continue as Earnings Arrive.

The company continues to face litigation over its talc baby powder, taken off the market in 2023.

Netflix Aims to Join the $1 Trillion Club

The streaming service has shared financial performance targets with staff that underscore its dominance.

Taco Bell's Australian Operator Plans to Exit the Business

Taco Bell's Australian franchise operator is exiting the business after the Mexican fast-food chain failed to meet its expectations.

Nvidia's Made-in-the-U.S.A. Move Follows a Familiar Trump Pattern

The chip maker's plan to build AI tech in Texas comes on the heels of a new tariff threat.

Lowe's Bets on Pro Market in Billion-Dollar ADG Acquisition

The company is aiming to grow its business with professional contractors.

Meta Fights to Keep Instagram and WhatsApp, as Antitrust Trial Begins

The Federal Trade Commission says Meta has monopolized the social-media market-and should be broken up.

Self-Driving Truck Startup Kodiak to Go Public in $2.5 Billion SPAC Deal

Deal comes amid slow market for U.S. IPOs as several firms work-and sometimes struggle-to automate long-haul trucking.

Luxury Empire LVMH Posts Revenue Below Views Amid Global Trade War Threat

French luxury behemoth LVMH said it booked revenue of EUR20.31 billion for the first three months of the year, 3% lower organically than in the prior-year period.

Intel to Sell Majority Stake of Altera to Private Equity Firm Silver Lake

Intel will sell a majority stake of its programmable-chip unit to private-equity firm Silver Lake.

data.title: LME copper firms on US tariff reprieve, China stimulus bets
data.description:
London copper firmed on Tuesday, holding near the more-than-one-week high hit in the previous session, aided by recent U.S. tariff exemptions and optimism that top consumer China will launch additional stimulus measures to bolster economic growth.

The benchmark three-month copper on the London Metal Exchange (LME) was up 0.1% at $9,192.5 a metric ton, as of 0252 GMT. In the previous session, the contract hit its highest level since April 4.

The most-traded copper contract on the Shanghai Futures Exchange (SHFE) rose 0.3% to 76,050 yuan ($10,402.42) per ton.

U.S. excluded smartphones and other electronics from its tariffs on China over the weekend, and after U.S. President Donald Trump added new wrinkles to his vacillating trade policy on Monday by suggesting he might grant exemptions on auto-related levies already in place.

However, the White House announced investigations into whether imports of pharmaceuticals and semiconductors threaten national security, potentially leading to tariffs on these products.

Copper gained after Trump handed out exemptions to his reciprocal tariffs. The pause on import fees on consumer electronics provided a temporary respite for markets after Trump's trade policies sowed deep uncertainty, said Daniel Hynes, a senior commodity strategist at ANZ Bank.

Meanwhile, China's imports of unwrought copper and copper products in March declined 1.4% to 467,000 tons from a year earlier, data from the General Administration of Customs showed on Monday.

Market participants are expecting more stimulus measures from Chinese authorities to bolster consumption and cushion the economic impact of an escalating trade war with the United States.

Goldman Sachs on Monday reduced its forecast for aluminium prices this year after its economists downgraded global growth forecasts, including for the U.S. and China.

SHFE aluminium eased 0.6% to 19,600 yuan a ton, zinc lost 0.4% to 22,385 yuan, lead retreated 0.8% to 16,780 yuan, nickel was up 0.8% at 123,960 yuan, tin fell 0.2% to 259,170 yuan.

LME aluminium gained 0.3% to $2,381.5 a ton, lead fell 0.4% to $1,909.5, tin was up 0.6% at $31,450, zinc was steady at $2,635 and nickel rose 0.4% to $15,365 a ton.

($1 = 7.3108 Chinese yuan)

data.title: CAR's Diversification Protects Against U.S. ChallengesMarket Talk
data.description: Australian vehicle advertiser CAR Group keeps its bull at Jefferies despite its exposure to a potentially challenging U.S. consumer environment. Analyst Roger Samuel highlights CAR's resilience amid macro uncertainty, telling clients in a note that the business is the most diversified it has ever been. The U.S. recreational vehicle industry backdrop is challenging, but Samuel thinks it is highly unlikely that dealers there would turn off subscriptions to CAR's Trader Interactive platform. The platform's growth in media, trucks and boats should also help. Jefferies cuts the stock's target price by 9.2% to A$39.70 on a higher risk premium, but keeps a buy rating. Shares are down 1.5% at A$32.22. (stuart.condie@wsj.com)

Hong Lai Huat Appoints COO
data.description:
Hong Lai Huat appointed Ong Jia Ming as the company's chief operating officer, effective May 1, according to a filing with the Singapore Exchange on Monday.

Huat will contribute to the company's local and regional growth in his new role, the filing added.

data.title: Mankind Pharma Gets Board Nod to Form Two Subsidiaries
data.description:
Mankind Pharma on Monday received board approval to incorporate two new subsidiaries, according to a same-day disclosure to the Indian stock exchanges.

The company proposes to form a wholly-owned subsidiary, directly or indirectly, in Russia, and another wholly-owned subsidiary in India for undertaking corporate social responsibilities activities of the Mankind Group.

The Russian subsidiary will carry out the distribution of pharmaceutical products of the company's wholly-owned subsidiary, Bharat Serums and Vaccines. Mankind has earmarked an initial investment of $5 million in the new unit that will be invested in one or more tranches.

Mankind Pharma also proposes to make an initial investment of 500,000 Indian rupees in the CSR-focused new subsidiary.

data.title: Sika Sees 2025 Sales Growth in Local Currencies of 3%-6% >SIKA.EB
Sika Sees Over-Proportional Increase in Ebitda in 2025>SIKA.EB
Sika Confirms 2028 Medium-Term Targets for Sustainable, Profitable Growth >SIKA.EB
Hong Kong Equities Subdued
data.description:
Hong Kong shares were little changed in early trade on Tuesday, hovering near 21,420, as gains in property and consumer stocks were offset by losses in tech and financials.

Traders weighed the impact of a temporary U.S. tariff exemption on popular electronics, announced by President Trump last Friday, while considering the possibility of new levies, including on semiconductors, in the coming weeks.

Investors also looked ahead to key Chinese economic data due later this week, including Q1 GDP, industrial production, retail sales, and the jobless rate.

Meanwhile, markets tracked Beijing’s ongoing efforts to stabilize mainland equities, with regulators reportedly capping daily net sales by hedge funds and major retail investors at CNY 50 million.

Among the top performers were Tingyi (Cayman Islands) Hlds. (4.0%), Pop Mart Intl. (3.6%), and CK Hutchison Hlds. (3.2%).

Notable decliners included Prada SpA (-5.7%), Hansoh Pharmaceutical (-3.3%), and Li Auto (-2.8%).

data.title: Sika reports first quarter sales of $3.28 billion, up 1.1%
data.description:
Swiss construction chemicals maker Sika on Tuesday reported a 1.1% rise in its first quarter sales to 2,678.3 million Swiss francs ($3.28 billion) and confirmed its outlook for 2025.

($1 = 0.8161 Swiss francs)

data.title: Indonesia Car Sales Fall 5.1% YoY in February
data.description:
Car sales in Indonesia declined by 5.1% year-on-year to 70,909 units in February 2025, reversing a 2.2% growth in the previous month.

The Association of Indonesian Automotive Industries (GAIKINDO) has set a car sales target for 2025 between 750,000 and 900,000 units.

This forecast surpasses the 2024 revised projection of 850,000 units and exceeds the previous year's actual sales, which reached 865,723 units wholesale and 889,680 units retail.

On a monthly basis, car sales fell by 1.9% in February 2025, following a 16.9% surge in the previous month.

data.title: Indonesia Consumer Mood Falls to 5-Month Low
data.description:
Indonesia's consumer confidence dropped to 121.1 in March 2025, down from February’s 126.4, declining for the third consecutive month amid weakening purchasing power and a shrinking middle class.

This marked the lowest reading since last October, as all the six sub-indices deteriorated: job availability (down 8.3 points to 125.9), job availability compared to six months ago (down 3.5 points to 100.3), economic outlook (down 7.0 points to 131.7), the country's current economic conditions (down 3.6 points to 110.6), income expectations for the next six months (down 6.3 points to 137.0), and income expectations for current income (down 1.4 points to 121.3) improved.

data.title: Sika 1Q Sales Grew 1.1% on Year >SIKA.EB
Sika 1Q Sales CHF2.68B >SIKA.EB
Analysts Saw Sika 1Q Sales at CHF2.6B>SIKA.EB
UBS lowers forecast for China 2025 GDP growth to 3.4% on tariff hikes
data.description:
UBS has downgraded its China GDP growth forecast to 3.4% for 2025, on the assumption that tariff hikes between it and the United States will remain in place and that Beijing will roll out additional stimulus, it said in a report on Tuesday.

The Swiss investment bank's previous forecast for China's growth this year was 4%. It maintained its 2026 forecast at 3%.

The bank also expected China's exports to the U.S. to fall by two-thirds in the coming quarters and overall Chinese exports to fall by 10% in U.S. dollar terms in 2025, by also factoring in slower American and global economic growth.

"We think some of China's other trading partners may also raise tariffs on Chinese goods in the coming months, but likely only on specific products and not in similar magnitudes as the U.S. tariffs," it said.

UBS said it was extremely difficult to predict how the tariffs between the U.S. and China could evolve, but said it is still possible that the world's two largest economies could engage in discussions and negotiations, and for both to "roll off some of the recent tariff hikes in the next month or two".

data.title: Sika Confirms 2025 Outlook, but Points to Increased Market Uncertainties Due to Trade Conflicts >SIKA.EB
Talius Group Signs MoU With South Australia Retirement Village Operator
data.description:
Talius Group signed a memorandum of understanding with retirement village operator Lifestyle SA and CareWindow, according to a Tuesday filing with the Australian bourse.

Under the MoU, the parties agreed to negotiate a formal master services agreement (MSA) for the upgrade and installation of assistive technology, which incorporates the Talius platform to improve care and connectivity across residents and villages, the filing said.

The parties agreed to start a pilot at one village subject to the finalization of the MSA, according to the filing.
